917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).

1004	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
1005	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
1006	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by
1007	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}.

1010	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
1011	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
1012	  This is the case \(n = 0\).

1015	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1016	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1017	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)

1019	  \[
1020	    (R^n G)(A)
1021	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1023	      \;\cong\; \cdots

21	\section{Setup and motivation}
22	
23	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
24	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
25	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
26	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
27	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
28	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
29	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
30	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
31	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
32	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
33	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
34	
35	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
36	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
37	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
38	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
39	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
40	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
41	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
42	\[
43	  \begin{array}{ccc}
44	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
45	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
46	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
47	  \end{array}
48	\]
49	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
50	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
51	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
52	
53	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
54	
55	\begin{definition}
56	
57	\leanok
58	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
59	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
61	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
62	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
63	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma

65	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
66	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
67	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.

70	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate

75	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
76	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
77	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
78	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
79	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
80	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
81	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
82	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
83	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
84	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
85	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
86	  \[
87	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
88	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
89	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
90	  \]
91	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
92	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
93	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
94	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
95	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
96	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
97	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
98	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
99	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
100	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
101	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
102	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
103	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
104	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
105	\end{definition}
106	
107	\begin{definition}
108	
109	\leanok
110	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
111	  \label{def:cech_complex}
112	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
113	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
114	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
115	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
116	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
118	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
119	  % is identified with the complex
120	  % $$
121	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
122	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
123	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
124	  % \ldots
125	  % $$
126	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
127	  % \begin{equation}
128	  % \label{equation-extended}
129	  % 0 \to
130	  % M \to
131	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
132	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
133	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
134	  % \ldots
135	  % \end{equation}
136	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
137	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
138	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
139	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
140	  vanishing).}
141	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
142	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
143	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
144	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
145	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
146	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
147	  in degree \(p\) it is
148	  \[
149	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
150	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
151	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
152	  \]
153	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
154	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
155	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
156	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
157	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
158	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
159	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
160	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
161	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
162	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
163	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
164	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
165	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
166	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
167	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
168	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
169	\end{definition}
170	
171	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
172	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
173	
174	It is convenient to factor the construction of
175	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
176	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
177	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
178	
179	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
180	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
181	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
182	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
183	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
184	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
185	\[
186	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
187	    \;=\;
188	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
189	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
190	\]
191	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
192	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
193	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
194	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
195	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
196	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
197	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
198	
199	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
200	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
201	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
202	\[
203	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
204	  \qquad
205	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
206	\]
207	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
208	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
209	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
210	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
211	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
212	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
213	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
214	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
215	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
216	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
217	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
218	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
219	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
220	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
221	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
222	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
223	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
224	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
225	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
226	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
227	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
228	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
229	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
230	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
231	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
232	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
233	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
234	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
235	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
236	
237	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
238	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
239	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
240	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
241	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
242	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
243	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
244	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
245	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
246	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
247	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
248	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
249	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
250	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
251	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
252	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
253	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
254	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
255	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
256	
257	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
258	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
259	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
260	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
261	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
262	
263	\begin{definition}\leanok
264	[Arrow of an affine cover]
265	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
266	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
267	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
268	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
269	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
270	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
271	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
272	  apply.
273	\end{definition}
274	
275	\begin{definition}[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
276	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOver, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOverAug}
278	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
279	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
280	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
281	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
282	  \[
283	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
284	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
285	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
286	  \]
287	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
288	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
289	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
290	\end{definition}

293	[Push--pull object map]
294	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
295	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
296	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
297	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
298	  \[
299	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},

300	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
301	  \]

305	\begin{definition}[Push--pull comparison map]
306	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
308	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
309	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
310	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
311	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
312	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
313	  \[
314	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
315	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
316	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
317	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
318	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
319	      \;=\;
320	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
321	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
322	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
323	  \]
324	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
325	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
326	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
327	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
328	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
329	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
330	\end{definition}

332	\begin{lemma}
333	
334	\leanok
335	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
336	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
337	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
338	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
339	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
340	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
341	\end{lemma}
342	
343	\begin{proof}
344	
345	\leanok
346	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
347	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
348	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
349	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
350	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
351	\end{proof}
352	
353	\begin{lemma}
354	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
355	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
356	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_pentagon}
357	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
358	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
359	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
360	  \[
361	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
362	  \]
363	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
364	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
365	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
366	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
367	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
368	\end{lemma}
369	
370	\begin{proof}
371	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
372	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
373	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
374	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
375	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
376	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
377	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
378	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
379	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
380	\end{proof}

382	\begin{lemma}
383	
384	\leanok
385	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
386	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
387	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
388	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
389	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
390	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
391	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
392	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
393	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
394	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
395	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
396	  \[
397	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
398	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
399	    \;=\;
400	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
401	  \]
402	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
403	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
404	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
405	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
406	\end{lemma}
407	
408	\begin{proof}
409	
410	\leanok
411	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
412	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
413	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
414	\end{proof}

417	
418	\leanok
419	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]

425	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
426	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
427	  collapses to the transport-light form

429	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
430	      \;\circ\; e_2
431	    \;=\;
432	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
433	      \;\circ\; e_3,
434	  \]
435	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by

437	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
438	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
439	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
440	  composition (pentagon) law.
441	\end{lemma}

443	\begin{proof}
444	
445	\leanok
446	  Substitute the over-triangle equality \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\) to replace
447	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
448	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.
449	\end{proof}

467	  functor is contravariant in the over-category \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\); it is the lift of the

471	\begin{definition}
472	
473	\leanok
474	[{\v C}ech nerve cosimplicial object]
475	  \label{def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial}

478	  The (non-augmented) cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module obtained by transporting the

481	  \(G\) of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_functor}: degreewise, the \(p\)-th term is the

487	\end{definition}

489	\begin{definition}\leanok
490	[Relative {\v C}ech complex from a nerve]
491	  \label{def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
492	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.relativeCechComplexOfNerve}
493	  \uses{def:cech_nerve}
494	  Given \(f : X \to S\) and an augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
495	  \(N\), the \emph{relative {\v C}ech cochain complex} in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)
496	  obtained by forgetting the augmentation of \(N\), pushing the resulting
497	  cosimplicial object forward degreewise along \(f\), and taking the
498	  alternating-coface-map cochain complex. This passage uses only the preadditivity
499	  of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) and \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence; it is
500	  the coherence-free plumbing (3) that turns the nerve of
501	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} into the {\v C}ech complex.
502	\end{definition}

504	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
505	
506	\begin{definition}
507	[Standard affine cover from a spanning family]
508	  \label{def:standard_affine_cover}
509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}
510	  \mathlibok
511	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cover.Open}).}
512	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring (so \(R = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R,
513	  \mathcal{O})\)) and let \(s : \iota \to R\) be a family of elements that generate
514	  the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\). The
515	  \emph{standard affine open cover} of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) associated with
516	  \(s\) is the affine open cover whose index set is \(\iota\), whose \(i\)-th member
517	  is the basic open \(D(s_i)\), and whose \(i\)-th structure morphism is the map of
518	  affine schemes induced by the localisation homomorphism \(R \to R_{s_i}\); that
519	  is, in Mathlib notation the defining equation
520	  \(\bigl(\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\bigr)_f\, i =
521	  \operatorname{Spec.map}\bigl(\text{algebraMap}\; R\; (\operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i))\bigr)\),
522	  so that the sections over the \(i\)-th piece are the \emph{away} localisation
523	  \(R_{s_i} = \operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i)\) and the geometric piece is
524	  \(D(s_i)\). This is exactly the standard open covering
525	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_i D(s_i)\) by basic opens, packaged as a single
526	  cover object. It is the spanning-family bundle \((s, hs)\) that the affine
527	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.
528	\end{definition}
529	
530	\subsection{The categorical--module bridge (L1)}
531	\label{sec:cech_l1_bridge}
532	
533	The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
534	\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
535	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
536	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
537	concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
538	\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
539	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
540	restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
541	reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
542	underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
543	Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
544	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
545	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
546	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
547	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.
548	
549	\begin{definition}
550	
551	[Quasi-coherent sections over a basic open are an away localisation]
552	  \label{def:qcoh_sections_localized}
553	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcohSectionsAwayLocalized,
554	    AlgebraicGeometry.basicOpen_sprod,
555	    AlgebraicGeometry.iInf_fin_succ,
556	    AlgebraicGeometry.qcohRestriction_eq_comparison,
557	    AlgebraicGeometry.tP,
558	    AlgebraicGeometry.tU,
559	    AlgebraicGeometry.phiL,
560	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi,
561	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi_eq_phiL,
562	    AlgebraicGeometry.restr_bridge}
563	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:standard_affine_cover}
564	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
565	  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L701--707).
566	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
567	  % as an $R_f$-module.
568	  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
569	  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
570	  % are the maps
571	  % $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
572	  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
573	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
574	  items (4)--(5).}
575	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
576	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module, and write
577	  \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\) for its module of global
578	  sections. For every \(g \in R\), the restriction-of-sections map
579	  \(M = \mathcal{F}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g))\) exhibits the
580	  section group \(\mathcal{F}(D(g))\) as the away localisation \(M_g\) of \(M\); that
581	  is, this map is an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the multiplicative
582	  submonoid \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g\) of powers of \(g\). Moreover, for
583	  \(D(g_2) \subseteq D(g_1)\) the restriction map
584	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(g_1)) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g_2))\) is, under these identifications, the
585	  canonical localisation map \(M_{g_1} \to M_{g_2}\) (the unique \(R\)-linear map
586	  compatible with the localisation units). Item~(4) of Tag~01HV gives the first
587	  assertion for \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) (\(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\)
588	  as an \(R_g\)-module) and item~(5) gives the second (the restriction along
589	  \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\)).
590	
591	  The module-level input on the side of Mathlib is the development
592	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}: the comparison map
593	  \((\operatorname{tilde.toOpen} M\, (D(g))).\mathrm{hom}\) carries the instance
594	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g)\), and
595	  \(\operatorname{isUnit\_algebraMap\_end\_basicOpen}\) records that \(g\) acts
596	  invertibly on the sections over \(D(g)\); the restriction compatibility is
597	  \(\operatorname{tilde.toOpen\_res}\). These supply the statement \emph{verbatim} for
598	  the standard sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\). For an \emph{arbitrary} quasi-coherent
599	  \(\mathcal{F}\) the remaining step is the canonical comparison

600	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})}\), i.e.\
601	  that the counit \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism. This
602	  is \emph{not} yet available in Mathlib for a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\):
603	  Mathlib provides it only from a \emph{global} presentation
604	  (\(\operatorname{isIso\_fromTildeΓ\_of\_presentation}\)), whereas quasi-coherence
605	  supplies only \emph{local} presentation data on a cover. Globalising the local data
606	  on the affine \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) --- the affine equivalence
607	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \simeq \operatorname{Mod}(R)\), Stacks
608	  Tag~01I8 --- is the one genuine gap this definition records as a build target; it is
609	  the canonical missing affine equivalence and is self-contained.
610	
611	  Two combinatorial--geometric ingredients package the identification into the form
612	  consumed by Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}. First, the multi-index
613	  intersection identity
614	  \(\bigl(\bigcap_k D(s_{\sigma(k)})\bigr) = D\bigl(\prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\bigr)\) in
615	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R).\mathrm{Opens}\) (\(\operatorname{basicOpen\_sprod}\), proved
616	  by induction on the index length using the infimum-splitting helper
617	  \(\operatorname{iInf\_fin\_succ}\) and \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen\_mul}\)):
618	  it rewrites the \((p+1)\)-fold intersection over a multi-index \(\sigma\) as the single
619	  basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\), so the
620	  section group there is the away localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). Second, the restriction
621	  compatibility of item~(5) is made explicit: for a basic-open inclusion
622	  \(D(b) \subseteq D(a)\) the presheaf restriction map of \(\widetilde{M}\) between the
623	  two section groups \emph{is} the away-localisation comparison
624	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison}\) of the two localisation units
625	  (\(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\), by the uniqueness
626	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison\_unique}\) of a map of localised modules);
627	  this is the single differential brick that the {\v C}ech coface match of
628	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} feeds on.
629	
630	  The identification is packaged by a few helpers: the two localised-module endpoints
631	  \(\operatorname{tP}, \operatorname{tU}\) (the section groups
632	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) named as away localisations at the two indices
633	  involved in a face), the per-\(\sigma\) comparison
634	  \(\varphi_\sigma = \operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.iso}\) realised both as the bare
635	  localisation comparison \(\operatorname{phiL}\) and as its sheaf-level avatar
636	  \(\operatorname{phi}\) (their agreement is \(\operatorname{phi\_eq\_phiL}\)), and the
637	  accessor-bridge \(\operatorname{restr\_bridge}\) that exhibits the presheaf
638	  restriction map through these comparisons.
639	\end{definition}

641	\begin{lemma}
642	[Section {\v C}ech homology vanishes iff the localised complex is exact]
643	  \label{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}
644	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_homology_exact,
645	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts,
646	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.isUnit_powers,
647	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.mul,
648	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.of_dvd,
649	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison,
650	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_apply,
651	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp,
652	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp_apply,
653	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp_structure,
654	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_self,
655	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_unique,
656	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoeff,
657	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoeff_transport_eq_comparison,
658	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoface,
659	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechLocalized_exact,
660	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechPrepend,
661	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hcomm,
662	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hsh,
663	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hu,
664	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod,
665	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod_cons,
666	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod_succAbove_dvd}
667	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized, lem:section_cech_module_exact,
668	    lem:section_cech_product_equiv, lem:section_cech_objd_apply,
669	    lem:section_cech_coface_match, lem:section_cech_ab_exact}
670	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
671	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (the {\v C}ech complex is
672	  % the complex of localisations) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
673	  % L58--67).
674	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
675	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
676	  % is identified with the complex
677	  % $$
678	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
679	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
680	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
681	  % \ldots
682	  % $$"
683	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
684	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (identification with the
685	  complex of localisations).}
686	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
687	  R}\)-module and \(\mathcal{U}\) the standard cover associated with a spanning family
688	  \(s : \iota \to R\). Under the section identification of
689	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}, the section {\v C}ech complex
690	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
691	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} is isomorphic, as a cochain complex, to
692	  the concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules
693	  \[
694	    D^\bullet : \quad
695	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
696	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0,1\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
697	    \cdots,
698	    \qquad
699	    s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
700	  \]
701	  whose differential is the alternating sum of the canonical localisation maps. The
702	  intended top-level conclusion is the directed homology vanishing
703	  \[
704	    1 \leq p
705	    \;\Longrightarrow\;
706	    \operatorname{IsZero}\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
707	      \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p\bigr),
708	  \]
709	  which is the form the affine vanishing
710	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} (\(\operatorname{sectionCech\_affine\_vanishing}\))
711	  consumes; it is read off from positive-degree exactness of the underlying group
712	  homomorphisms,
713	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to D^{p+1}\bigr)\), which is
714	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.
715	
716	  The reduction from the abstract homology to this group-level exactness is \emph{not}
717	  a \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\) statement: the degree-\(p\) object of
718	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) is the \emph{categorical
719	  product}
720	  \(\prod^{c}_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}
721	  \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) in the category \(\mathrm{Ab}\) of abelian groups (the
722	  alternating-coface-map complex of the \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-valued cosimplicial object
723	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\)), not an \(R\)-module object, so the
724	  module-category criterion \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.moduleCat\_exact\_iff}\) does
725	  not apply. The correct chain runs through three steps, isolated as the sub-lemmas
726	  below: \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology is \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) at
727	  \(p\) (\(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\)); \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\)
728	  of a short complex in \(\mathrm{Ab}\) is the underlying-group exactness
729	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\) via the abelian
730	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
731	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}); and the categorical product is moved to the
732	  per-\(\sigma\) localised modules by the element-level product equivalence
733	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
734	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\)
735	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}), under which the {\v C}ech coface
736	  differential matches the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
737	  \(D^\bullet\) (the abstract cosimplicial unfold
738	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} followed by the tilde-bridge identification
739	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). Transporting the
740	  already-proved exactness \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of
741	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} across the product equivalence then
742	  discharges the group-level exactness. The active target is the tilde case
743	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), for which the per-coordinate comparisons are
744	  immediate from \(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\); a general
745	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) is reduced to this case via the affine equivalence
746	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma\mathcal{F}}\) (Stacks Tag~01I8), the
747	  separately-deferred globalisation gap recorded in
748	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}.
749	\end{lemma}
750	
751	\begin{proof}
752	  \uses{def:qcoh_sections_localized, def:section_cech_complex}
753	  The term identification is the degreewise statement of
754	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the degree-\(p\) section group
755	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is \(\prod_\sigma M_{s_\sigma}\), since
756	  each \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap
757	  D(s_{\sigma(p)})\) is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
758	  \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\) and the sections there are the away
759	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). The differential identification is item~(5) of
760	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: each face restriction
761	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})) \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is the
762	  canonical localisation map \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\), so the
763	  alternating sum of restrictions is the alternating sum of localisation maps, the
764	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) (the coface match of
765	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). The homology vanishing then follows in
766	  three steps. First, \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) turns
767	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology at \(p\) into \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of
768	  the short complex at \(p\). Second, because the degree-\(p\) objects are
769	  \emph{categorical products in \(\mathrm{Ab}\)} rather than \(R\)-modules, the abelian
770	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
771	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}) --- not the module-category criterion ---
772	  reduces \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) to the underlying-group exactness
773	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\). Third, the
774	  element-level product equivalence
775	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
776	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
777	  identifies each product group with the per-\(\sigma\) localised module \(D^p\), so the
778	  group-level exactness is exactly \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to
779	  D^{p+1})\), supplied by \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\)
780	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}).
781	
782	  The identification of the abstract section complex with the concrete localised
783	  complex \(D^\bullet\) --- together with the construction of the prepend transport
784	  \(c\) and the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\) discharging the unit, shift, and
785	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\)/\(\mathtt{hsh}\)/\(\mathtt{hcomm}\)
786	  of the dependent combinatorial port --- is the bulk of the bridge. The non-circular
787	  route is reflection along the localisation equivalence on its essential image (the
788	  global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is the inverse equivalence there, so it reflects
789	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of homology); it is \emph{not} the assertion that
790	  ``\(\Gamma\) preserves homology'', which would presuppose the very affine exactness
791	  being proved.
792	\end{proof}

794	\begin{lemma}
795	
796	[Element-level product equivalence for the section {\v C}ech terms]
797	  \label{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
798	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechProductEquiv,
799	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechProductEquiv_apply,
800	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionProdAddEquiv}
801	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
802	  Let \(\{F_\sigma\}_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}\) be the finite family of
803	  abelian groups \(F_\sigma = \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) appearing in degree \(p\) of
804	  the section {\v C}ech complex of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}. There is
805	  an equivalence of underlying types
806	  \[
807	    \operatorname{ToType}\Bigl(\prod^{c}_{\sigma} F_\sigma\Bigr)
808	    \;\simeq\;
809	    \prod_{\sigma} \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma),
810	  \]
811	  between the elements of the categorical product \(\prod^{c}_{\sigma} F_\sigma\) in
812	  \(\mathrm{Ab}\) and the dependent functions \(\sigma \mapsto\) (element of
813	  \(F_\sigma\)). It is supplied by the Mathlib concrete-limit comparison
814	  \(\operatorname{CategoryTheory.Limits.Concrete.productEquiv}\), which holds because the
815	  forgetful functor \(\operatorname{forget}\,\mathrm{Ab}\) preserves the discrete
816	  product (\(\operatorname{PreservesLimit}(\operatorname{Discrete.functor} F)\)). Under
817	  this equivalence each coordinate
818	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma)))\) is identified with the away
819	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma} = \operatorname{dCoeff}\) of
820	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} by the
821	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) uniqueness of
822	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} (both
823	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) and \(M_{s_\sigma}\) localise \(M\) at \(s_\sigma\), so
824	  the comparison is the canonical isomorphism). This is the move between the
825	  categorical product of \(\mathrm{Ab}\) and the per-\(\sigma\) localised modules.
826	  Its packaging as an additive isomorphism --- the degreewise vertical map of the
827	  ladder transport in Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact} --- is recorded as
828	  \(\operatorname{sectionProdAddEquiv}\), the \(\operatorname{AddEquiv}\) underlying
829	  the coordinatewise comparison.
830	  The coordinate projection of the equivalence is recorded separately as the
831	  definitional restatement
832	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv\_apply}\): the \(\sigma\)-component of the
833	  image of \(y\) under the equivalence is the underlying group map of the categorical
834	  projection \(\pi_\sigma\) applied to \(y\).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
839	  The type-level equivalence is \(\operatorname{Concrete.productEquiv}\) applied to the
840	  family \(\sigma \mapsto F_\sigma\); its hypothesis is that
841	  \(\operatorname{forget}\,\mathrm{Ab}\) preserves the discrete-diagram limit, which is
842	  the standard instance for \(\mathrm{Ab}\). The per-coordinate identification
843	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))) \cong M_{s_\sigma}\) is the
844	  uniqueness clause of \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\): by
845	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} the restriction map
846	  \(M \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) and the localisation unit
847	  \(M \to M_{s_\sigma}\) are both localisations of \(M\) at
848	  \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers}(s_\sigma)\)
849	  (\(\operatorname{qcohSectionsAwayLocalized}\)), hence canonically isomorphic over
850	  \(M\). Assembling the coordinatewise isomorphisms after the product equivalence gives
851	  the stated identification.
852	\end{proof}

854	\begin{lemma}
855	[Abstract cosimplicial unfold of the section {\v C}ech differential]
856	  \label{lem:section_cech_objd_apply}
857	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_objD_apply,
858	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechFaceRestr}

860	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
861	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open cover. For a multi-index
862	  \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(q+2) \to I\) and a face index
863	  \(i \in \mathrm{Fin}(q+2)\), write
864	  \[
865	    \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i) :
866	      \mathcal{F}\Bigl(\,\bigcap_{l} U_{(\sigma \circ d_i)(l)}\Bigr)
867	      \;\longrightarrow\;
868	      \mathcal{F}\Bigl(\,\bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)}\Bigr)
869	  \]
870	  for the \(i\)-th presheaf face restriction --- the value of \(\mathcal{F}\) on the
871	  inclusion of opens
872	  \(\bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)} \subseteq \bigcap_{l} U_{(\sigma \circ d_i)(l)}\)
873	  obtained from the order-preserving coface \(d_i\) of the simplex category. Then,
874	  read through the product equivalence
875	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}\) of
876	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}, the underlying group action of the
877	  degree-\(q\) differential
878	  \(\operatorname{objD} q\) of the alternating-coface-map complex
879	  \(\operatorname{AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex}
880	  (\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\,\mathcal{U}\,\mathcal{F})\) is the alternating
881	  sum of the face restrictions applied to the deleted-face coordinates:
882	  \[
883	    \bigl(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_{q+1}\,(\operatorname{objD} q\, t)\bigr)_\sigma
884	      \;=\;
885	      \sum_{i = 0}^{q+1} (-1)^i \,
886	        \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)
887	          \Bigl(\bigl(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_{q}\, t\bigr)_{\sigma \circ d_i}\Bigr).
888	  \]
889	  This is \emph{purely cosimplicial}: it records the shape of the section {\v C}ech
890	  differential as an alternating sum of presheaf restrictions, with \emph{no}
891	  sheaf-theoretic or localisation identification of those restrictions. It is the
892	  abstract shape that the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
893	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} matches once the tilde-bridge
894	  identification of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match} is supplied.
895	\end{lemma}

4431	  The action of the global-ring section functor \(\Gamma_R(-,\mathcal{F})\) is, by definition, the
4432	  restriction of scalars of \(\mathcal{F}\)'s structure-sheaf module action along the global-sections
4433	  identification \(\theta_R\). Unfolding that definition, the \(R\)-scalar \(r\) acts as the structure-sheaf
4434	  scalar \(\rho^{W}(\theta_R(r))\) on the unchanged underlying section; the equality therefore holds by
4435	  definitional unfolding.
4436	\end{proof}

4438	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4439	[Tile action transports definitionally to the ambient structure-sheaf action]
4440	  \label{lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq}
4441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq}
4442	  \uses{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}
4443	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}\)-module and let \(g \in R\). Write
4444	  \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) for the restricted module (tile) over \(\Spec R_g\) obtained as the iterated
4445	  restriction of \(\mathcal{F}\) along the affine identification \(\Spec R_g \cong D(g)\) followed by the
4446	  open inclusion \(D(g) \hookrightarrow \Spec R\). Let \(c \in \Gamma(\top, \mathcal{O}_{\Spec R_g})\) and
4447	  let \(m\) be a section of \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) over \(\top\). Then the tile action of \(c\) on \(m\)
4448	  transports to the ambient structure-sheaf action of \(\mathcal{F}\):
4449	  \[
4450	    c \cdot m \;=\; \bigl(\alpha_g^{-1}\bigl(\beta_g^{-1}(c)\bigr)\bigr) \cdot m_{\mathcal{F}},
4451	  \]
4452	  where \(\beta_g\) and \(\alpha_g\) are the ring isomorphisms on global sections induced respectively by
4453	  the affine identification \(\Spec R_g \cong D(g)\) and by the open inclusion \(D(g) \hookrightarrow
4454	  \Spec R\), and \(m_{\mathcal{F}}\) denotes \(m\) regarded as a section of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the
4455	  iterated-image open \(W \subseteq \Spec R\) (which equals \(D(g)\) by
4456	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}).
4457	\end{lemma}
4458	\begin{proof}
4459	  \uses{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}
4460	  \leanok
4461	  Restriction of a module along an open immersion is the pushforward whose scalar action is reindexed by
4462	  the inverse of the open immersion's global-sections ring map. The tile \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) is the
4463	  composite of two such restrictions --- along the affine identification \(\Spec R_g \cong D(g)\) and along
4464	  the open inclusion \(D(g) \hookrightarrow \Spec R\) --- so its action of \(c\) is, by definition, the
4465	  ambient \(\mathcal{F}\)-action of \(\alpha_g^{-1}(\beta_g^{-1}(c))\) on the unchanged underlying section.
4466	  The equality holds by definitional unfolding; keeping the \(\mathcal{F}\)-side open in the
4467	  iterated-image form \(W\) (rather than rewriting it to \(D(g)\)) is what makes the two carriers
4468	  coincide on the nose.
4469	\end{proof}
4470	
4471	\begin{lemma}
4472	[Tile action transports to the ambient action over an arbitrary open]
4473	  \label{lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq_genV}
4474	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq'}
4475	  \uses{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}
4476	  The general-open companion of Lemma~\ref{lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq}. Let \(\mathcal{F}\)
4477	  be an \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}\)-module, let \(g \in R\), and let \(V \subseteq \Spec R_g\) be an
4478	  \emph{arbitrary} open (the sibling treats only \(V = \top\)). For \(c \in \Gamma(V,
4479	  \mathcal{O}_{\Spec R_g})\) and a section \(m\) of the tile \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) over \(V\), the tile
4480	  action of \(c\) on \(m\) transports to the ambient structure-sheaf action of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the
4481	  iterated-image open \(\iota(V) \subseteq \Spec R\):
4482	  \[
4483	    c \cdot m \;=\; \bigl(\alpha_g^{-1}\bigl(\beta_g^{-1}(c)\bigr)\bigr) \cdot m_{\mathcal{F}},
4484	  \]
4485	  where \(\beta_g\) and \(\alpha_g\) are the section ring isomorphisms induced by the affine
4486	  identification \(\Spec R_g \cong D(g)\) and by the open inclusion \(D(g) \hookrightarrow \Spec R\)
4487	  (now read at the open \(V\) rather than at \(\top\)), and \(m_{\mathcal{F}}\) denotes \(m\) regarded
4488	  as a section of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the iterated-image open \(\iota(V)\).
4489	\end{lemma}
4490	\begin{proof}
4491	  \uses{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}
4492	  As in Lemma~\ref{lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq}, the tile \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) is the
4493	  composite of the two open-immersion restrictions, whose scalar action is by definition reindexed by
4494	  the inverses of the two section ring maps. At an arbitrary open \(V\) this reindexing is identical, so
4495	  the equality holds by definitional unfolding; keeping the \(\mathcal{F}\)-side open in the
4496	  iterated-image form \(\iota(V)\) (rather than rewriting it to a basic open) is what makes the two
4497	  carriers coincide on the nose.
4498	\end{proof}

4500	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4501	[Open-immersion section iso reads as a restriction]
4502	  \label{lem:appTop_appIso_inv_eq_res}
4503	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.appTop_appIso_inv_eq_res}
4504	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an open immersion of schemes. On global sections \(f\) induces the comparison
4505	  ring map \(f^{\sharp}_{\top} : \Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\), and the
4506	  open-immersion section isomorphism
4507	  \(\beta_f : \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Gamma\bigl(f(\top), \mathcal{O}_Y\bigr)\)
4508	  identifies the global sections of \(X\) with the sections of \(Y\) over the image open
4509	  \(f(\top) \subseteq Y\). Then the composite of \(f^{\sharp}_{\top}\) with \(\beta_f^{-1}\) is the
4510	  structure-sheaf restriction of \(Y\) from \(\top\) down to the image open:
4511	  \[
4512	    \beta_f^{-1} \circ f^{\sharp}_{\top} \;=\; \rho^{f(\top)}_{Y}.
4513	  \]
4514	  Equivalently, under the section identification \(\beta_f\) the global-sections comparison map of \(f\)
4515	  is nothing but restriction along \(f(\top) \hookrightarrow Y\).
4516	\end{lemma}
4517	\begin{proof}
4518	
4519	\leanok
4520	  The forward map of \(\beta_f\) is, by construction, the comparison map \(f^{\sharp}\) read on the image
4521	  open. Rewriting the identity in the form \(f^{\sharp}_{\top} = \rho^{f(\top)}_Y \circ \beta_f\) and
4522	  applying the naturality of the comparison maps \(f^{\sharp}\) with respect to the restriction maps of
4523	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and \(\mathcal{O}_Y\), the right-hand side reduces to \(f^{\sharp}_{\top}\)
4524	  precomposed with the identity restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) on \(\top\); the source open \(\top\)
4525	  and its image are matched by the thin-category uniqueness of open inclusions. This leaves exactly
4526	  \(\rho^{f(\top)}_Y\).
4527	\end{proof}

4529	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4530	[$\Gamma$--$\Spec$ naturality of the localisation immersion, section form]
4531	  \label{lem:key_morph}
4532	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.key_morph}
4533	  \uses{lem:appTop_appIso_inv_eq_res}
4534	  Let \(g \in R\), write \(R_g = R[g^{-1}]\), let \(\lambda : R \to R_g\) be the localisation map, and let
4535	  \(\iota = \Spec(\lambda) : \Spec R_g \to \Spec R\) be the induced affine morphism (it is the open
4536	  immersion identifying \(\Spec R_g\) with \(D(g) = \iota(\top)\)). Then the restriction to the image
4537	  open \(D(g)\) of the global-sections identification
4538	  \(\theta_R : R \xrightarrow{\sim} \Gamma(\Spec R, \mathcal{O})\) factors as the localisation map
4539	  followed by the global-sections identification of \(\Spec R_g\) and the (inverse) section isomorphism
4540	  of \(\iota\):
4541	  \[
4542	    \rho^{D(g)} \circ \theta_R
4543	    \;=\;
4544	    \beta_\iota^{-1} \circ \theta_{R_g} \circ \lambda,
4545	  \]
4546	  where \(\theta_{R_g} : R_g \xrightarrow{\sim} \Gamma(\Spec R_g, \mathcal{O})\) is the global-sections
4547	  identification of \(\Spec R_g\) and \(\beta_\iota\) is the open-immersion section isomorphism of
4548	  \(\iota\) at \(\top\).
4549	\end{lemma}
4550	\begin{proof}
4551	  \uses{lem:appTop_appIso_inv_eq_res}
4552	  \leanok
4553	  This is the naturality of the \(\Spec\)-to-global-sections comparison applied to the ring map
4554	  \(\lambda\). Concretely, the Mathlib naturality square for the global-sections identification (the
4555	  \(\Gamma\)-\(\Spec\) adjunction unit, \texttt{ΓSpecIso} naturality) gives
4556	  \(\lambda \) followed by \(\theta_{R_g}\) equals \(\theta_R\) followed by the comparison map
4557	  \(\iota^{\sharp}_{\top}\) of \(\iota = \Spec(\lambda)\). Substituting this into the right-hand side and
4558	  reading the composite of \(\iota^{\sharp}_{\top}\) with \(\beta_\iota^{-1}\) as the restriction to the
4559	  image open \(D(g)\) by Lemma~\ref{lem:appTop_appIso_inv_eq_res} yields \(\rho^{D(g)} \circ \theta_R\).
4560	\end{proof}

4562	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4563	[Composition of the two tile section isomorphisms]
4564	  \label{lem:tile_appIso_comp}
4565	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tile_appIso_comp}
4566	  In the situation of Lemma~\ref{lem:key_morph} the localisation immersion \(\iota\) factors as the
4567	  composite of the affine identification \(\Spec R_g \xrightarrow{\sim} D(g)\) (of schemes) with the open
4568	  inclusion \(D(g) \hookrightarrow \Spec R\). The (inverse) open-immersion section isomorphisms of these
4569	  two factors compose into the single (inverse) section isomorphism \(\beta_\iota^{-1}\) of \(\iota\) at
4570	  \(\top\), up to a structure-sheaf transport along the equality of image opens
4571	  \(\iota(\top) = D(g)\):
4572	  \[
4573	    \beta_{\hookrightarrow}^{-1} \circ \beta_{\mathrm{aff}}^{-1}
4574	    \;=\;
4575	    \tau \circ \beta_\iota^{-1},
4576	  \]
4577	  where \(\beta_{\mathrm{aff}}\) and \(\beta_{\hookrightarrow}\) are the section isomorphisms of the
4578	  affine identification and of the open inclusion respectively, and \(\tau\) is the structure-sheaf
4579	  transport induced by the equality of the composed image open with \(D(g)\).
4580	\end{lemma}
4581	\begin{proof}
4582	
4583	\leanok
4584	  The functoriality of the open-immersion section isomorphism under composition of open immersions
4585	  (\texttt{comp\_appIso}) expresses \(\beta_\iota\) as the composite of the two factor isomorphisms, with
4586	  the section isomorphism of the open inclusion being the identity. Folding the resulting transport
4587	  through the equality of image opens \(\iota(\top) = D(g)\) gives the displayed identity, with \(\tau\)
4588	  the structure-sheaf map of that equality of opens.
4589	\end{proof}

4591	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4592	[Structure-sheaf ring identity of the tile comparison, morphism form]
4593	  \label{lem:tile_section_ring_identity}
4594	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tile_section_ring_identity}
4595	  \uses{lem:key_morph, lem:tile_appIso_comp}
4596	  In the situation of Lemma~\ref{lem:key_morph}, the restriction to the tile image open \(D(g)\) of the
4597	  global-sections identification \(\theta_R\) of \(\Spec R\) equals the localisation map followed by the
4598	  global-sections identification of \(\Spec R_g\) and the two (inverse) open-immersion section
4599	  isomorphisms of the tile:
4600	  \[
4601	    \rho^{D(g)} \circ \theta_R
4602	    \;=\;
4603	    \beta_{\hookrightarrow}^{-1} \circ \beta_{\mathrm{aff}}^{-1} \circ \theta_{R_g} \circ \lambda.
4604	  \]
4605	  This is the morphism-level (ring-map) form of the residual ring identity of
4606	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_comparison}.
4607	\end{lemma}
4608	\begin{proof}
4609	  \uses{lem:key_morph, lem:tile_appIso_comp}
4610	  \leanok
4611	  Substitute the \(\Gamma\)-\(\Spec\) naturality identity of Lemma~\ref{lem:key_morph}, which rewrites
4612	  \(\rho^{D(g)} \circ \theta_R\) as \(\beta_\iota^{-1} \circ \theta_{R_g} \circ \lambda\), and then
4613	  replace the single section isomorphism \(\beta_\iota^{-1}\) by the composite of the two tile factor
4614	  isomorphisms using Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_appIso_comp}. The structure-sheaf transport \(\tau\) of
4615	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_appIso_comp} merges with the restriction maps in the thin category of opens
4616	  (functoriality of the structure presheaf), and the two displayed sides coincide.
4617	\end{proof}

4619	\begin{lemma}
4620	[Structure-sheaf ring identity of the tile comparison at an arbitrary open]
4621	  \label{lem:tile_section_ring_identity_genV}
4622	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tile_section_ring_identity', AlgebraicGeometry.appIso_inv_res,
4623	    AlgebraicGeometry.appIso_inv_res_assoc}
4624	  \uses{lem:tile_section_ring_identity}
4625	  The general-open companion of Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_ring_identity}. For an \emph{arbitrary}
4626	  open \(V \subseteq \Spec R_g\), the restriction to the iterated-image open \(\iota(V)\) of the
4627	  global-sections identification \(\theta_R\) of \(\Spec R\) equals the localisation map followed by the
4628	  global-sections identification of \(\Spec R_g\), the restriction to \(V\), and the two (inverse)
4629	  open-immersion section isomorphisms of the tile read at \(V\):
4630	  \[
4631	    \rho^{\iota(V)} \circ \theta_R
4632	    \;=\;
4633	    \beta_{\hookrightarrow,V}^{-1} \circ \beta_{\mathrm{aff},V}^{-1} \circ \rho^{V}_{R_g} \circ \theta_{R_g} \circ \lambda.
4634	  \]
4635	  This supplies the ring identity at the target open \(V = D(\bar f)\) consumed by the general-open
4636	  scalar reconciliation Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_scalar_compat_genV}.
4637	\end{lemma}
4638	\begin{proof}
4639	  \uses{lem:tile_section_ring_identity}
4640	  Derive from the \(V = \top\) case (Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_ring_identity}) by post-composing both
4641	  sides with the structure-sheaf restriction \(\iota(V) \le \iota(\top)\) and pushing that restriction
4642	  through the two open-immersion section isomorphisms. The push-through is the section-restriction form
4643	  of the open-immersion section-iso naturality: for an open immersion \(f\) and opens \(U' \le U\), the
4644	  inverse section iso at \(U\) followed by the \(Y\)-restriction \(f(U') \le f(U)\) equals the
4645	  \(X\)-restriction \(U' \le U\) followed by the inverse section iso at \(U'\). The residual restriction
4646	  \(\rho^{\top}_{R_g}\) on \(\Spec R_g\) is the identity by
4647	  uniqueness of morphisms in the thin category of opens.
4648	\end{proof}

4650	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4651	[Scalar compatibility of the tile comparison at the source open]
4652	  \label{lem:tile_scalar_compat}
4653	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tile_scalar_compat}
4654	  \uses{lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq, lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq,
4655	    lem:tile_section_ring_identity}
4656	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}\)-module, let \(g, r \in R\), and let
4657	  \(x\) be a section of the affine tile \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) over \(\top\) --- equivalently, regarding
4658	  the underlying carrier, a section of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the tile image open \(D(g)\). Then the native
4659	  \(R\)-action of \(r\) on \(x\) (through \(\Gamma_R(-,\mathcal{F})\)) coincides with the \(R_g\)-action
4660	  of the localised scalar \(\lambda(r) \in R_g\) on \(x\) (through the tile
4661	  \(\Gamma_{R_g}(-, \mathcal{F}_{(g)})\)):
4662	  \[
4663	    r \cdot x \;=\; \lambda(r) \cdot x,
4664	    \qquad \lambda : R \to R_g \text{ the localisation map}.
4665	  \]
4666	  This is the scalar core, at the source open \(V = \top\), of the section comparison of
4667	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_comparison}; it is the scalar-tower compatibility \(R \to R_g\) on the
4668	  common underlying section carrier. It is \emph{not} the full natural isomorphism, only the equality of
4669	  the two scalar actions on the single open \(V = \top\).
4670	\end{lemma}
4671	\begin{proof}
4672	  \uses{lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq, lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq,
4673	    lem:tile_section_ring_identity}
4674	    \leanok
4675	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq} the native \(R\)-action of \(r\) on \(x\) is the
4676	  structure-sheaf action of \(\rho^{D(g)}(\theta_R(r))\), and by
4677	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq} the \(R_g\)-action of \(\lambda(r)\) on \(x\) (as a
4678	  tile section) transports to the structure-sheaf action of
4679	  \(\beta_{\hookrightarrow}^{-1}(\beta_{\mathrm{aff}}^{-1}(\theta_{R_g}(\lambda(r))))\) on the same
4680	  underlying carrier. Both actions therefore reduce to a structure-sheaf scalar action, and the two
4681	  scalars agree by the morphism-level ring identity of Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_ring_identity}
4682	  evaluated at \(r\).
4683	\end{proof}

4686	[Scalar compatibility of the tile comparison at an arbitrary open]
4687	  \label{lem:tile_scalar_compat_genV}
4688	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tile_scalar_compat'}

4694	  \(V\) --- equivalently, on the underlying carrier, a section of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the
4695	  iterated-image open \(\iota(V)\). Then the native \(R\)-action of \(r\) on \(x\) coincides with the
4696	  \(R_g\)-action of the localised scalar \(\lambda(r) \in R_g\) on \(x\):
4697	  \[
4698	    r \cdot x \;=\; \lambda(r) \cdot x,
4699	    \qquad \lambda : R \to R_g \text{ the localisation map}.
4700	  \]
4701	  The instance at \(V = D(\bar f)\) is the scalar-tower compatibility \(R \to R_g\) at the \emph{target}
4702	  open of the per-tile section localization (Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_localization}); the

4827	\begin{lemma}
4828	[Per-tile section localisation at \(f\)]
4829	  \label{lem:tile_section_localization}
4830	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tile_section_localization}
4831	  \uses{lem:qcoh_finite_presentation_cover, lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen,
4832	    lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation, lem:restrict_obj_mathlib,
4833	    lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_restrictScalars_of_algebraMap,
4834	    lem:tile_image_opens_identities, lem:tile_scalar_compat,
4835	    lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq, lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq}
4836	  Let \(X = \Spec R\), let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, let \(f \in R\),
4837	  and let \(g \in R\) be either a cover element \(g = g_j\) or an overlap product \(g = g_j g_k\) of the
4838	  finite presentation cover of Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_finite_presentation_cover}, so that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) of
4839	  \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(\Spec R_g\) admits a global presentation. Then the restriction-of-sections map
4840	  \(\Gamma(D(g), \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\) exhibits its target as the localisation of
4841	  its source at the powers of \(f\); equivalently, there is a canonical \(R\)-linear isomorphism
4842	  \(\Gamma(D(g), \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\).
4843	\end{lemma}
4844	\begin{proof}
4845	  \uses{lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen, lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation,
4846	    lem:restrict_obj_mathlib, lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_restrictScalars_of_algebraMap,
4847	    lem:tile_image_opens_identities, lem:tile_scalar_compat,
4848	    lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq, lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq}
4849	  % NOTE (review iter-045): proof \leanok REMOVED — the Lean decl is absent (commented-out sketch only,
4850	  % blocked on W1–W3); see the statement-block NOTE above.
4851	  % NOTE: the naive route using the carrier equality of Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib}
4852	  % is UNSOUND: that equality holds only for the local-ring section functor,
4853	  % whereas the localisation here uses the global-ring section functor,
4854	  % which does NOT commute with restriction definitionally. Hence the five-step descent below.
4855	  Let \(\iota : \Spec R_g \xrightarrow{\sim} D(g) \hookrightarrow X\) be the open immersion of
4856	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}, with \(\bar f\) the image of \(f\) under the
4857	  localisation map \(R \to R_g\). The proof is a base-ring descent in five steps; the section
4858	  comparison it rests on is \emph{not} the definitional equality of
4859	  Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib} (see the proof of
4860	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_comparison}).
4861	
4862	  \emph{Step 1 (global presentation of the tile).} Since \(D(g)\) lies in a presentation member of the
4863	  cover, step B4 (Lemma~\ref{lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen}) presents the tile
4864	  \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) globally over \(\Spec R_g\).
4865	
4866	  \emph{Step 2 (localisation over \(R_g\)).} Apply
4867	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation} to the globally presented
4868	  \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) and \(\bar f\): the section-restriction map
4869	  \(\Gamma_{R_g}(\Spec R_g, \mathcal{F}_{(g)}) \to \Gamma_{R_g}(D(\bar f), \mathcal{F}_{(g)})\)
4870	  exhibits its target as a localisation at the powers of \(\bar f\), \emph{as \(R_g\)-modules}. Note this
4871	  is over \(R_g\), not yet over \(R\).
4872	
4873	  \emph{Step 3 (opens identities).} By Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_image_opens_identities} the two image opens of
4874	  \(\iota\) are \(\iota(\Spec R_g) = D(g)\) and \(\iota(D(\bar f)) = D(gf) = D(g) \cap D(f)\).
4875	
4876	  \emph{Step 4 (the section comparison, as a restriction of scalars).} The localisation of Step 2 lives
4877	  over \(R_g\); it must be related to the section-restriction map of \(\mathcal{F}\) over \(R\). The two
4878	  maps share the same underlying map of section sets: this is the carrier identification of
4879	  Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib}, now read as an equality of \(R\)-linear maps (with the
4880	  \(\mathcal{F}\)-side open kept in its iterated-image form \(W = \iota(V)\)). The one genuine difference
4881	  is the base ring: the tile sections carry an \(R_g\)-module structure, while the
4882	  \(\mathcal{F}\)-sections carry an \(R\)-module structure. The descent that reconciles them is precisely
4883	  the \emph{restriction of scalars} along the localisation map \(R \to R_g\): each tile section module,
4884	  viewed through restriction of scalars, becomes an \(R\)-module, and the Step-2 map so viewed is the
4885	  section-restriction map \(\Gamma_R(D(g), \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma_R(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\).
4886	
4887	  For this restriction of scalars to be meaningful, the native \(R\)-action on each section must agree
4888	  with its \(R_g\)-action read through \(R \to R_g\); this is the scalar-tower compatibility. At the
4889	  source open \(V = \top\) (image \(D(g)\)) it is exactly Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_scalar_compat}, whose two
4890	  scalar prescriptions agree by the scalar-reduction identities
4891	  (Lemmas~\ref{lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq} and \ref{lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq}). At the
4892	  target open \(V = D(\bar f)\) (image \(D(gf)\)) it is the general-open companion
4893	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_scalar_compat_genV}, the \(V = D(\bar f)\) instance of the same ring-naturality
4894	  content. Both opens of the Step-2 map are thus covered.
4895	
4896	  Reading Step 2's localisation-at-\(\bar f\) property through restriction of scalars along
4897	  \(R \to R_g\), and matching source and target opens by Step 3, yields: the map
4898	  \(\Gamma_R(D(g), \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma_R(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\) exhibits its target as a localisation
4899	  at the powers of \(\bar f\), \emph{still as \(R_g\)-modules}.
4900	
4901	  \emph{Step 5 (base-ring descent to \(R\)).} Finally descend the base ring from \(R_g\) to \(R\) by
4902	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_restrictScalars_of_algebraMap}, applied with \(A = R_g\) and the
4903	  scalar tower \(R \to R_g\) on the two section modules: since \(\bar f\) is the image of \(f\) under
4904	  \(R \to R_g\), the powers of \(\bar f\) descend to the powers of \(f\), and the restriction of scalars
4905	  (from \(R_g\) to \(R\)) of the Step-4 map exhibits \(\Gamma_R(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\) as the localisation
4906	  of \(\Gamma_R(D(g), \mathcal{F})\) at the powers of \(f\) \emph{over \(R\)}. Equivalently there is a
4907	  canonical \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(\Gamma(D(g), \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\).
4908	
4909	  Crucially this localisation is carried out entirely on the tile \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\), where
4910	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is associated to a module (the tile is globally presented, hence of the form
4911	  \(\widetilde{M_g}\)), and never on
4912	  the global object \(\mathcal{F}\). This is exactly the structural feature that keeps the keystone
4913	  Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule} non-circular: the keystone-at-\(g\) statement is
4914	  \emph{not} invoked; only the presented-tile lemma is.
4915	\end{proof}

4917	\begin{lemma}[Kernel comparison: the localised global equalizer matches the \(D(f)\)-cover equalizer]
4918	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_kernel_comparison}
4919	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcoh_section_kernel_comparison}
4920	  \uses{lem:qcoh_finite_presentation_cover, lem:qcoh_section_equalizer,
4921	    lem:localized_module_map_exact_mathlib, lem:tile_section_localization}
4922	  Let \(X = \Spec R\), let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, let \(f \in R\),
4923	  and let \(g_1, \ldots, g_n\) be the finite presentation cover of
4924	  Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_finite_presentation_cover}, with \(\operatorname{span}\{g_j\} = R\). Then the
4925	  canonical \(R\)-linear map \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) induced by
4926	  \(\rho_f : \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) (the localisation lift of \(\rho_f\)
4927	  at the powers of \(f\)) is an isomorphism.
4928	\end{lemma}
4929	\begin{proof}
4930	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_equalizer, lem:localized_module_map_exact_mathlib,
4931	    lem:tile_section_localization}
4932	  Write the degree-\(0/1\) equalizer (Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_section_equalizer}) for \(\mathcal{F}\) on the
4933	  standard cover \(X = \bigcup_j D(g_j)\) (take \(W = X\)):
4934	  \[
4935	    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \xrightarrow{\rho} \prod_j \Gamma(D(g_j), \mathcal{F})
4936	      \xrightarrow{\delta} \prod_{j,k} \Gamma(D(g_j g_k), \mathcal{F}),
4937	  \]
4938	  and the same equalizer for the induced cover \(D(f) = \bigcup_j \bigl(D(f) \cap D(g_j)\bigr)\) of
4939	  \(D(f)\) (take \(W = D(f)\), using \(D(f) \cap D(g_j) = D(g_j f)\)):
4940	  \[
4941	    0 \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F}) \xrightarrow{\rho'} \prod_j \Gamma(D(g_j f), \mathcal{F})
4942	      \xrightarrow{\delta'} \prod_{j,k} \Gamma(D(g_j g_k f), \mathcal{F}).
4943	  \]
4944	  Localise the first sequence at the powers of \(f\). Localisation is exact
4945	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:localized_module_map_exact_mathlib}) and commutes with the finite products, so
4946	  \[
4947	    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \xrightarrow{\rho_f} \prod_j \Gamma(D(g_j), \mathcal{F})_f
4948	      \xrightarrow{\delta_f} \prod_{j,k} \Gamma(D(g_j g_k), \mathcal{F})_f
4949	  \]
4950	  is exact; it exhibits \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f\) as the kernel of \(\delta_f\) (and \(\rho_f\) here
4951	  is the localisation lift of the restriction product, hence the localisation lift of \(\rho_f\) on its
4952	  first component).
4953	
4954	  For every cover element \(g_j\) and overlap product \(g_j g_k\), the per-tile localisation
4955	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_localization}) supplies canonical \(R\)-linear isomorphisms
4956	  \[
4957	    \Gamma(D(g_j), \mathcal{F})_f \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Gamma(D(g_j f), \mathcal{F}),
4958	    \qquad
4959	    \Gamma(D(g_j g_k), \mathcal{F})_f \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Gamma(D(g_j g_k f), \mathcal{F}),
4960	  \]
4961	  hence isomorphisms of the product terms
4962	  \(\prod_j \Gamma(D(g_j), \mathcal{F})_f \cong \prod_j \Gamma(D(g_j f), \mathcal{F})\) and
4963	  \(\prod_{j,k} \Gamma(D(g_j g_k), \mathcal{F})_f \cong \prod_{j,k} \Gamma(D(g_j g_k f), \mathcal{F})\).
4964	  These isomorphisms intertwine the overlap differentials,
4965	  \(\delta' \circ (\text{iso}) = (\text{iso}) \circ \delta_f\): both composites carry a localised cover
4966	  section to its restriction on the overlap tile, and the two routes agree because restriction along the
4967	  inclusion \(D(g_j g_k f) \subseteq D(g_j g_k)\) and localisation at \(f\) are induced by one and the
4968	  same ring map. This is the degree-\(0/1\) naturality of restriction-versus-localisation. The same
4969	  square commutes for the first maps: under the cover-term identification \(\rho' = (\text{iso}) \circ
4970	  \rho_f\), because restricting a global section to \(D(g_j)\) and then localising at \(f\) equals first
4971	  applying \(\rho_f\) and then restricting to \(D(g_j f)\).
4972	
4973	  Thus the two left-exact sequences are isomorphic from the cover term onward by isomorphisms commuting
4974	  with the differentials. Passing to kernels, the induced map
4975	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) is an isomorphism, and by the commuting
4976	  first square it is precisely the canonical map induced by \(\rho_f\). Hence that map is an
4977	  isomorphism.
4978	\end{proof}

4333	\begin{lemma}[Localisation preserves exactness]
4334	  \label{lem:localized_module_map_exact_mathlib}
4335	  \lean{IsLocalizedModule.map_exact}
4336	  \mathlibok
4337	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
4338	  Let \(S \subseteq R\) be a submonoid of a commutative ring and let
4339	  \(M_0 \xrightarrow{\,a\,} M_1 \xrightarrow{\,b\,} M_2\) be \(R\)-linear maps forming an exact
4340	  sequence at \(M_1\) (the image of \(a\) equals the kernel of \(b\)). Fix, for each \(i\), a
4341	  localisation map \(\ell_i : M_i \to M_i'\) at \(S\) (so each \(M_i'\) is the localised module
4342	  \(S^{-1} M_i\)). Then the induced maps \(S^{-1} a : M_0' \to M_1'\) and
4343	  \(S^{-1} b : M_1' \to M_2'\) again form an exact sequence at \(M_1'\). In other words,
4344	  localisation at \(S\) is an exact functor; in particular it carries short exact sequences to short
4345	  exact sequences and commutes
4346	  with the formation of kernels.
4347	\end{lemma}
